bull_thesis: Liberty Global is a holding company whose shares trade far below the combined worth of the businesses it owns, and there is now a board-approved, credibly-sponsored event designed to close that gap. A holding company (a parent that owns stakes in operating businesses rather than running them directly) often trades at a discount to its sum-of-the-parts value (the worth of each business line added up separately). On February 18, 2026 the board announced it will carve out Ziggo Group, combining VodafoneZiggo and Telenet into one company with roughly 13 million customers and about EUR6.6 billion in revenue, and list it on the Euronext Amsterdam exchange in 2027, distributing 90% of the shares to Liberty Global holders. Management is running the same playbook it already pulled off with the Sunrise spin-off completed in late 2024, so this is not a new experiment: it has done exactly this, recently, and closed it. The enabling step, buying Vodafone's 50% of the VodafoneZiggo joint venture for EUR1.0 billion in cash plus a 10% Ziggo stake, is expected to close in the second half of 2026. The stock trades at about 0.41 times book value and roughly a $4 billion market value, on businesses targeting about EUR500 million of adjusted free cash flow by 2028 and bringing leverage down to about 4.5 times. In other words, the market is pricing in a steep conglomerate discount that a clean, separately-listed, index-eligible Ziggo plus a simplified leftover Liberty Global is built to erase. Steady buybacks shrink the share count each quarter, which lifts the per-share value over time. As the Vodafone purchase closes and the 2027 listing gets a firm date, the discount should narrow toward the true asset value.
bear_thesis: The spin-off is a 2027 window, not a fixed record or effective date, and that horizon of roughly 378 days is a long way off. Management itself hedged the timing, saying it will be able to forecast the storyline only once it gets into the second half of next year. The whole thesis also hinges on a regulatory step that must clear first: the Vodafone joint-venture buy-in, slated for the second half of 2026, could attract an EU second-request review or other antitrust drag and push the entire timeline back. The underlying assets are leveraged European cable and telecom businesses facing fiber overbuild and customers shifting to mobile, a slow squeeze on the valuation multiple that caps how far the spun-out company can re-rate, so even a clean separation may not fully close the gap to book value. If the discount simply persists, which is common for European holding companies, and the catalyst slips, the stock keeps trading as an overlooked, leveraged cable holding company near its 52-week low of about $9.73. With leverage currently above the 4.5 times target set for 2028, there is refinancing and interest-rate sensitivity in the meantime.
sop_bull: If the event plays out, the value is around $18.00, a deliberately conservative re-rate as the spin-off de-risks. The math: value a separately-listed Ziggo Group (about EUR6.6 billion in revenue, roughly 13 million subscribers) on a typical regional-cable EV/EBITDA multiple (enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, the standard yardstick for these assets), then add the leftover Liberty Global, which includes the Telenet remainder, the VMO2 joint-venture stake, the Liberty Growth ventures portfolio, the already-separated Sunrise stub, and net liquidity, sum it all, and narrow the discount. The Street and management base case sits closer to $27, and book value is about $28-plus (price-to-book of 0.41), so $18 is a haircut on top of a haircut, leaving room above if the discount narrows even part of the way toward true asset value. Ongoing buybacks shrink the float and lift that per-share figure over the holding period.
sop_bear: The verified downside floor is about $9.50. The math: the standalone equity value of the leveraged operating assets if no re-rate happens and the holding-company discount sticks, roughly the 52-week low of $9.73 in a world where the deal slips and the discount stays put. It is backed by real cash flows, with about EUR500 million of adjusted free cash flow targeted by 2028, and a continuous buyback that supports per-share value even without the spin-off. There is no question about the company surviving; this is a story of leverage plus discount, not insolvency, so the floor is genuine and comfortably above zero.
sop_breakdown: This is a probability-weighted base case. The verified target is $18.00, which assumes the event resolves, Ziggo is listed and spun out on Euronext Amsterdam in 2027 and the discount narrows, and even that is conservative against the roughly $27 Street and management sum-of-the-parts figure and the $28-plus book value. The verified floor is $9.50, the deal-slips-and-discount-persists case near the 52-week low. We weight it roughly 60% to the target and 40% to the floor, reflecting a board-approved event with a real precedent in the 2024 Sunrise deal, but tempered by a soft 2027 date and a still-pending second-half-2026 regulatory step: 0.6 times $18.00 plus 0.4 times $9.50 equals $14.60, rounded to about $14.50. We lean conservative given how far off the horizon is.
risk_reward: Upside to the target is plus $5.99, about 50%, against downside to the floor of minus $2.51, about 21%, from roughly $12.01, for a reward-to-risk ratio of about 2.39. The asymmetry is even wider measured against the true-asset-value target, the roughly $27 Street sum-of-the-parts and $28 book value, where upside exceeds 100%.
catalyst_summary: The board has approved combining VodafoneZiggo and Telenet into Ziggo Group and listing it on Euronext Amsterdam in 2027, with 90% of the shares distributed to holders. It is gated on the Vodafone joint-venture buy-in closing in the second half of 2026. The setup offers about 50% upside to an $18 target against a real floor of about $9.50, a reward-to-risk ratio of 2.39, with the 2024 Sunrise deal as precedent that lowers execution risk.
dated_milestone: The Vodafone VodafoneZiggo joint-venture buy-in, EUR1.0 billion in cash plus a 10% Ziggo stake, is expected to close in the second half of 2026, subject to regulatory approval. The Ziggo Group listing on Euronext Amsterdam and the 90% spin-off are targeted for 2027 (per the board's 8-K filing dated February 18, 2026).
forcing_function: The catalyst that forces the re-rating comes in two steps: first, the second-half-2026 regulatory close of the Vodafone joint-venture purchase, the first firm gate, and then the 2027 Euronext Amsterdam listing and record date for the Ziggo Group spin-off, along with the index-inclusion buying that follows. That corporate event is what should collapse the holding-company discount.
consensus_delta: Here is where we disagree with the market. The Street still anchors Liberty Global to its long-running holding-company discount and its leveraged-European-cable image, a price-to-book of 0.41 and a roughly $4 billion market value, treating the spin-off as just another piece of perpetually-deferred Liberty financial engineering. The mispricing: the board has named the structure (Ziggo Group), the venue (Euronext Amsterdam), the year (2027), and the mechanism (a 90% distribution), and it has the 2024 Sunrise deal proving it actually closes these, yet the price reflects almost none of the unlock. Even a conservative $18 re-rate is a discount to the roughly $27 Street sum-of-the-parts and $28 book value. Consensus is stuck on discount forever, against a sponsor with a live, dated path to execution.
valley_of_death: The risky window is the next 3 to 9 months. The Vodafone joint-venture buy-in must clear regulators to close in the second half of 2026, and an EU second-request review or antitrust drag is the main risk that could slip it and defer the whole spin-off. Interim leverage sits above the 4.5 times target set for 2028, leaving some refinancing and interest-rate sensitivity. There is no cash burn or covenant cliff flagged; the risk is a timeline slip and a lingering discount, not insolvency. The stock could drift toward the 52-week low of about $9.73 if the close is delayed.
positioning_washout: This is not a dividend or yield washout. Liberty Global returns capital through buybacks rather than a dividend, so there is no mechanical exodus of income-focused holders. The relevant flow runs the other way: a 2027 Euronext listing of Ziggo Group creates fresh index-inclusion buying and a clean local investor base, while the simplified leftover Liberty Global draws holding-company and sum-of-the-parts specialists. That is a positive forced-flow event, not a forced sell-off.
moderator_conclusion: This is a verified, board-approved, precedent-backed unlock of the sum-of-the-parts value, with genuine 2.39 reward-to-risk asymmetry and a real, non-zero floor. But it is a setup to stage into, not one to buy in full right now. The catalyst is a hard, dated event in form, the structure, venue, and mechanism are all named, and the sponsor has already run the identical Sunrise playbook, yet the spin-off itself is a 2027 window, a soft date roughly 378 days out, and it is gated on a second-half-2026 regulatory close. That far-off, condition-first timeline is exactly the kind of setup where you wait for forced selling: the right move is to build the position gradually and add once the Vodafone close confirms and 2027 gets a firm listing or record date. The call is to wait for the wash-out, with conviction 4, a strong, credible event one milestone short of imminent. Value conviction is also 4: the roughly $9.50 floor against a $12 price and a roughly $28 book value gives a real margin of safety, held back from a 5 only by the eroding moat and the gradual secular drift in European cable on the underlying assets, which is manageable since the listing and index unlock are largely independent of it.
interrogator_dossier:
# LBTYK - Liberty Global Ltd. | Catalyst Dossier (Event-Driven Special Situation)

**Frame:** This is not a judgment about business quality or compounding. Liberty Global is a European cable and telecom holding company trading at a deep, structural discount to the combined worth of its parts. The thesis is the gap between the live price (about $11.6 to $12.0) and the value unlocked when the Benelux operating businesses are listed and distributed. The franchise looks cheap-and-mediocre (price-to-book about 0.41, leveraged, exposed to cord-cutting and fiber overbuild) by design; the catalyst is what carries the thesis.

## 1. THE EVENT - Is it real, dated, and binding?
Verified. The board announced, in an 8-K filing dated February 18, 2026, the formation of Ziggo Group, combining VodafoneZiggo (Netherlands) and Telenet (Belgium), roughly 13 million customers and about EUR6.6 billion ($7.7 billion) in revenue, to be listed on Euronext Amsterdam in 2027, with 90% of the shares spun off to Liberty Global shareholders (subject to shareholder approval). This is a real, board-approved corporate action with a named structure, named venue, and named year.

Enabling step (the hard regulatory gate): Liberty Global signed a definitive agreement to acquire Vodafone's 50% of the VodafoneZiggo joint venture for EUR1.0 billion in cash plus a 10% stake in Ziggo Group. This buy-in is expected to close in the second half of 2026, subject to regulatory approvals. It must clear before the spin-off proceeds, and it is the first firm, dated milestone.

Sponsor credibility: Liberty Global ran the materially identical playbook with the Sunrise spin-off completed in late 2024, separating the Swiss unit and distributing it to shareholders. Management, Fries and Mason, has a multi-decade record of unlocking value through splits, spin-offs, and buybacks. The Sunrise precedent is the strongest credibility anchor: they have done exactly this, recently, and successfully.

## 2. THE FLOOR - What backstops the price if the event fails?
The downside is bounded by the standalone equity value of the leveraged operating assets plus the leftover holding company (the already-separated Sunrise stub, Telenet, the VMO2 joint-venture stake, the ventures and Liberty Growth portfolio, and net liquidity). At about 0.41 times book value and a roughly $4 billion market value, on businesses targeting about EUR500 million of adjusted free cash flow by 2028 with leverage falling to about 4.5 times by 2028, the equity already prices in a steep holding-company discount. The $9.50 floor lines up with the 52-week low of $9.73 and a world where the deal slips, no re-rate happens, and the discount persists. It is real because the cash flows and the aggressive buyback (a continuous shrinking of the float) provide ongoing per-share support even without the spin-off. The floor is not zero; there is no question about the company surviving. This is leverage plus discount, not insolvency.

## 3. THE ASYMMETRY
- Live: about $12.01 (current quote $11.60 to $11.64)
- Target (conservative, event-resolved re-rate): $18.00
- Floor (deal slips, discount persists): $9.50
- Upside plus $5.99 (about 50%); downside minus $2.51 (about 21%); reward-to-risk about 2.39.
- The target is itself conservative: the Street and management sum-of-the-parts base case is about $27, and book value is about $28-plus (price-to-book 0.41). The $18 is a haircut on top of a haircut, leaving room if the discount narrows even part of the way toward true asset value, which widens the asymmetry.

## 4. RED FLAGS / RISKS
- Concern: The date on the spin-off itself is soft. The 2027 listing is a year ("as we get into the second half of next year"), not a fixed record or effective date, and the roughly 378-day horizon is far off. This argues for watching and staging in, not buying now.
- Concern: Regulatory contingency. The Vodafone buy-in (closing in the second half of 2026) is a condition that must clear first; an EU second-request review or antitrust drag would slip the whole timeline.
- Concern: Leverage. The roughly 4.5 times target is for 2028, so today's level is higher, carrying interest-rate and refinancing risk on the operating-company debt, though holding-company ring-fencing limits the worst-case risk.
- Concern: Secular pressure. European cable faces fiber overbuild and customers shifting to mobile, which compresses the multiple the spun-out company can earn and caps the re-rate, but does not impair the event itself: a separately-listed Ziggo with index inclusion is the unlock regardless.
- Positive: Buybacks. Aggressive float shrinkage is a continuous per-share tailwind that supports the floor and compounds the eventual per-share sum-of-the-parts.
- Positive: Precedent. The 2024 Sunrise deal proves management closes these.

## 5. VERDICT
The event is real and board-approved, with a credible serial-spinner sponsor and a verified standalone floor. It is not yet firmly dated (the 2027 listing is a window, far off) and is gated on a second-half-2026 regulatory close, making this a pending, staging setup rather than an imminent one. The asymmetry is genuine (2.39 reward-to-risk on a conservative target, more on the true-asset-value target). The right posture is to watch and stage in; the catalyst strengthens as the Vodafone close lands and the 2027 listing gets a firm date.

Management credibility 4. Trajectory: strengthening. The moat is narrow, with an eroding trend, and the secular threat is manageable. of 5.
skeptic_kill_fact: The catalyst is independently verified as real and board-approved, but it is not a closed arbitrage spread with a hard floor. The spin-off is a 2027 listing window with no fixed record or effective date, gated on a regulatory close that is still pending (the second half of 2026, subject to approvals, and unclosed as of June 2026). Most important, the $9.50 floor is a holding-company-discount and sum-of-the-parts construct (near the 52-week low of $9.73), not a deal-break price or a cash-recovery backstop. This is a voluntary self-restructuring, so there is no merger-break floor and no acquirer's cash bid sitting underneath the stock. If the European holding-company discount simply persists, which is the chronic base rate for these names, or the regulatory close slips, nothing mechanically supports the price. The asymmetry survives as a staging setup, not a buy-now closed spread.

catalyst.dated_milestone: Vodafone VodafoneZiggo JV buy-in (EUR1.0bn cash + 10% Ziggo stake) expected to close H2 2026, subject to regulatory approval; Ziggo Group Euronext Amsterdam listing + 90% spin-off targeted for 2027 (board 8-K Feb 18, 2026).
catalyst.binding_reason: The board has approved combining VodafoneZiggo and Telenet into Ziggo Group, listing it on Euronext Amsterdam, and distributing 90% to holders. The sponsor is credible, having executed the identical Sunrise 2024 playbook, and the setup offers about 2.39-to-1 to an $18 target against a real floor of about $9.50, set against a roughly $28 book value. The skeptic verified the case at a cap of 55, but the spin-off is a 2027 window with no fixed record or effective date, gated on an unclosed second-half-2026 regulatory buy-in, and the floor is a discount-persists sum-of-the-parts construct with no deal-break or cash backstop, on a European-cable base whose moat is eroding. Stage in on the Vodafone close.
